pages.title: Psychology | Sacramento State
pages.description: The Psychology Department at California State University, Sacramento provides its students with the scientific study of human and animal behavior, cognition, and emotion.
headings: Psychology College of Social Sciences & Interdisciplinary Studies
contentBlocks: To educate, research, and practice in the field of Psychology with dedication and enthusiasm. We facilitate students’ intellectual and personal growth. We prepare students for the workforce, graduate studies, community responsibilities, and life demands. We advance the many areas of our discipline through active and creative scholarship. We serve diverse communities through meaningful collaborations with people and organizations. Through teaching, scholarship, and service, we promote equity, health, well-being, and respect for diversity.

The discipline of psychology is the scientific study of mind and behavior. It includes a broad range of research areas, research methodologies, and practical applications. Our faculty represent diverse areas of expertise as represented in our programs and course offerings.
Now accepting Lecturer Pool applications
Applying to Psychology - Fall 2026 & Beyond
Admission Requirements & Application Instructions
MA in Industrial-Organizational Psychology
Participating in Research for Course Credit

The Department of Psychology is committed to promoting diversity, equity and inclusion within the department, our school, our community, and the field of psychology. We seek to address barriers to social equity and social justice to ensure all students, staff, and faculty can thrive and achieve their personal, academic, and professional goals.
Psychology Department Amador Hall, Room 350 Monday – Friday, 8 a.m. – 5 p.m.
Phone: (916) 278-6254 psychology@csus.edu

contentBlocks: Fully declared PSYC majors, minors, and students adding a PSYC minor are eligible to receive major advising from faculty. Appointment scheduling is conducted through EAB and overseen by each faculty member. Email the faculty member for appointment scheduling support.

headings: Psychology College of Social Sciences & Interdisciplinary Studies
For any student currently enrolled in a course with one of the faculty members listed.
contentBlocks: These timeslots are subject to cancellation depending on the faculty availability. Please email faculty directly to inquire about their current availability. This list includes all faculty instructing a course during the present term including Tenure-Track, Lecturer, and TA positions.
Note that only Tenure-Track faculty can provide major advising .

pages.title: Prospect Peer Mentoring for Success | Sacramento State
pages.description: Learn more about the Prospects team that offers mentoring to help Psychology majors achieve success in their degree.
headings: Psychology College of Social Sciences & Interdisciplinary Studies
contentBlocks: Prospects is made up of a team of experienced and knowledgeable Psychology majors who are trained as Peer Mentors. Our goal is to help guide students toward their academic and social goals throughout the Psychology Program.
Watch this promotional video to hear from our current mentors and mentees!
Explore the different subfields of psychology
Learn about the recommended courses for specific concentrations
Find internship and research assistant opportunities
Apply to various psychology-related clubs
Receive assistance with any other psychology-related issues
Understand University policies and procedures
Join PSYC 198A, a 1-unit (CR/NC), semester-long course where you’ll build a meaningful mentor-mentee relationship while exploring the field of psychology, professionalism, and campus resources.
Apply to become a Mentee ! Mentee applications are accepted year-round.
If selected and paired with a mentor, you’ll be contacted 1-2 weeks before the start of the semester to confirm your interest.
No need to register for the class yourself - the Psychology department will add the 1-unit class to your schedule within the first two weeks of the semester.
Join PSYC 198B, a 3-unit graded course where you will mentor two students, develop professionalism and leadership skills, and receive training in resume building and multicultural diversity.

Submit a Mentor Interest Survey to read more about the application process and receive emails for upcoming deadlines. Mentor interest surveys are accepted year-round. You’re also welcome to visit during office hours to speak with a current Mentor or email us your questions.

contentBlocks: Students transferring from a California community college should use assist.org to determine course equivalencies that satisfy Sacramento State Psychology prerequisite requirements.

Students transferring coursework from a four-year or out-of-state institution must schedule an appointment with the Department Chair (Dr. Furtak) via psychology@csus.edu . Students are required to provide the course syllabus and/or the following details at the time of the meeting:
Indication of whether the course is designated as upper-division or lower-division.
PSYC 143-01: Practicum in Community Psychology Practicum in Community Psychology Application priority deadline April 25, 2026
Enrollment in these upper division courses listed below require faculty initiated enrollment via a Special Problems form and are not available for student self-enrollment.
Special Problems enrollment form processing is changing for Fall 2026 registration. Psychology Faculty will have access to the form through the department Canvas portal.
Our student organizations are a great way to meet new friends, dive deeper into psychology, and give/receive mentorship.

pages.title: Admission Requirements & Application Instructions | Sacramento State
pages.description: This page provides information about applying to the Sacramento State Psychology Graduate Program.
headings: Psychology College of Social Sciences & Interdisciplinary Studies
International Applicants and Domestic Applicants with Foreign Documents
Do you have a Counseling/MFT program?
Do you have a Doctoral Program?
Do I need to take the GRE?
How do I submit proof of meeting the Stats/Methods requirement?
How can I check on the status of my application?
I am an international student; how do I submit my application?
contentBlocks: Preferred Cumulative Undergraduate GPA of 3.0. Applicants with GPAs between 2.5 and 2.99 will be given lower priority.
Undergraduate degree in Psychology preferred. ABA program applicants generally have a BA or BS in Psychology or a related field, such as Child Development, Speech Pathology, or Education.

Program-specific Coursework Requirements. Applied Behavior Analysis All applicants must have completed a lower division undergraduate psychology course in research methods and an upper division undergraduate course in ABA with at least “C” grades in those courses. The lower division research requirement is met by PSYC 8 at Sacramento State. All ABA Applicants are recommended to have prior upper division coursework in Applied Behavior Analysis prior to applying to the program. This could be achieved by taking PSYC 171 at Sacramento State, or an equivalent at another university. Applicants without Applied Behavior Analysis coursework will still be considered but may be asked to take PSYC 171 during their first year of graduate school. Industrial-Organizational Psychology Lower Division Preparation. Lower division preparation is met with the following list of classes at Sacramento State, or their equivalents at a community college or 4-year university: Introductory Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 2); Methods of Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 8); and Statistics for Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 9). Statistics and Methods Preparation. All applicants must have completed two (2) upper division statistics/methods courses at a 4-year college with grades of C or better. This requirement is met with PSYC 121 and PSYC 102 at Sacramento State, or their equivalents at other universities. Course equivalents must have included research methods in which students have analyzed a dataset and written an APA-style paper. They must also have included descriptive and complex inferential statistics, such as factorial ANOVAs, mixed model ANOVAs, ANCOVAs, MANOVAS, planned and unplanned comparisons, factorial analysis, and multiple regression. Courses without these elements will not be approved. *PSYC 121 must be completed by the application deadline. PSYC 102 can be in progress to be admitted to the program or completed in the first semester of the program. Upper Division Preparation. Applicants must take five Psychology courses at a 4-year university such as: Industrial Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 169), Organizational Psychology (Sac State: Psyc 108), Psychological Measurement (Sac State: Psyc 120), Learning (Sac State: PSYC 104), Motivation (Sac State: PSYC 106), Social Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 145). Psychological Research Statistics and Method. All applicants must have completed two (2) upper division statistics/methods courses at a 4-year college with grades of C or better. This requirement is met with PSYC 121 and PSYC 102 at Sacramento State, or their equivalents at other universities. Course equivalents must have included research methods in which students have analyzed a dataset and written an APA-style paper. They must also have included descriptive and complex inferential statistics, such as factorial ANOVAs, mixed model ANOVAs, ANCOVAs, MANOVAS, planned and unplanned comparisons, factorial analysis, and multiple regression. Courses without these elements will not be approved. * PSYC 121 must be completed by the application deadline. PSYC 102 can be in progress to be admitted to the program or completed in the first semester of the program. Lower Division Preparation. The following courses may be taken at a community college or 4 -year university: Introductory Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 2); Methods of Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 8); and Statistics for Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 9). Upper Division Preparation. Applicants must take five Psychology courses at a 4-year university such as: Perception (Sac State: PSYC 103); Cognitive Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 110); Social Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 145); Abnormal Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 168); Developmental Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 148, PSYC 149, or PSYC 150); etc.

Applied Behavior Analysis All applicants must have completed a lower division undergraduate psychology course in research methods and an upper division undergraduate course in ABA with at least “C” grades in those courses. The lower division research requirement is met by PSYC 8 at Sacramento State. All ABA Applicants are recommended to have prior upper division coursework in Applied Behavior Analysis prior to applying to the program. This could be achieved by taking PSYC 171 at Sacramento State, or an equivalent at another university. Applicants without Applied Behavior Analysis coursework will still be considered but may be asked to take PSYC 171 during their first year of graduate school.

Courses without these elements will not be approved. * PSYC 121 must be completed by the application deadline. PSYC 102 can be in progress to be admitted to the program or completed in the first semester of the program.

Lower Division Preparation. The following courses may be taken at a community college or 4 -year university: Introductory Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 2); Methods of Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 8); and Statistics for Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 9).

Upper Division Preparation. Applicants must take five Psychology courses at a 4-year university such as: Perception (Sac State: PSYC 103); Cognitive Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 110); Social Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 145); Abnormal Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 168); Developmental Psychology (Sac State: PSYC 148, PSYC 149, or PSYC 150); etc.
The Sacramento State Psychology Graduate Program only accepts applicants for fall admission. There is no spring admission.
The following are our application and letter of recommendation deadlines. Late applications will not be accepted.
University Application period begins October 1, 2025.
University Application due – January 15, 2026
Letters of Recommendation due – January 15, 2026

International Applicants and Domestic Applicants with foreign documents must submit their application to the University no later than December 15, 2025 to ensure verification of their transcripts. International Applicants please contact the International Programs and Global Education office for assistance. Domestic Applicants with foreign documents should consult the Office of Graduate Studies prior to submitting their documents.
Complete a CSU System Application via CalState Apply.

Send one set of official transcripts from all colleges and universities attended, except Sacramento State. Transcripts should be addressed to: gradtranscripts@csus.edu (preferred). Physical copies can be addressed to: Office of Graduate Studies California State University, Sacramento Riverfront Center, Room 215, MS 6112 6000 J St. Sacramento, CA 95819

In Quadrant IV, you will be required to upload the following information: Personal Statement Specific program option requirements (see below) One (1) PDF copy of unofficial transcripts from ALL colleges and universities attended, INCLUDING Sacramento State. 3 Letters of Recommendation Catalog descriptions and syllabi for courses used to meet program prerequisites.
Specific program option requirements (see below)
One (1) PDF copy of unofficial transcripts from ALL colleges and universities attended, INCLUDING Sacramento State.
Catalog descriptions and syllabi for courses used to meet program prerequisites.
To check the status of a Cal State Apply application, contact Graduate Studies at (916) 278-6470.

Submitted in Quadrant IV in CalState Apply Application. Please carefully review the following information as it relates to the additional items required by each program. Failure to submit these additional materials may result in a denied application.

A personal statement of professional goals and experiences (max 2 double spaced pages). Include information on previous research and practicum experiences, academic training in ABA (if any), and post-graduation professional goals. Applicants should demonstrate that they are committed to studying Behavioral Analysis as a science to understand human behavior.

Provide a list of faculty within the behavior analysis program with whom you would like to work and why. Research some of the ABA faculty’s academic publications to become familiar with their research interests.
A Curriculum Vitae (CV). List any behavior analysis classes with grades.
A minimum of three letters of recommendation (two must be from former professors).

Indicate whether you will be able to attend a personal interview/program tour on campus, to be held on TBA. Applicants are asked to RSVP on the Department application. A remote interview option is available.

Each student works closely with a faculty mentor who supervises their research. Please provide a ranked list of three proposed faculty mentors within the Psychological Research Master’s program with whom you would like to work.

A personal statement (max 2 double spaced pages) describing the following: Experience: Describe any research, fieldwork, and/or other academic experiences that have prepared you for graduate education in your chosen area of study. Goals: Describe your post-graduation professional goals. Research Interests: Describe your research interests. Research Interest Overlap: Describe how your research interests overlap with each of your proposed faculty mentors’ areas of expertise .Tip: Read some of your proposed faculty mentors’ academic publications to become familiar with their research interests.
Experience: Describe any research, fieldwork, and/or other academic experiences that have prepared you for graduate education in your chosen area of study.
Goals: Describe your post-graduation professional goals.
Research Interests: Describe your research interests.

Research Interest Overlap: Describe how your research interests overlap with each of your proposed faculty mentors’ areas of expertise .Tip: Read some of your proposed faculty mentors’ academic publications to become familiar with their research interests.
A Curriculum Vitae (CV) providing an overview of your professional and educational background and relevant skills.
A writing sample (a sole authored research report, research proposal, and/or literature review); this can be from an undergraduate course.
Please do not use ChatGPT or any form of generative AI (i.e., the use of artificial intelligence technology to produce content) to write your personal statement or writing sample.
Students who apply to the I-O program will be asked to write a personal statement that is a maximum of three double-spaced pages, in which they address the following questions:
Why are you interested in the field of Industrial-Organizational Psychology? Briefly describe the topics that you would like to study while in graduate school.
What academic background (e.g., I-O related coursework and/or research experience) has prepared you for a graduate program in Industrial-Organizational Psychology?
What work experiences (e.g., internships, volunteer work, full or part-time jobs) have prepared you to study Industrial-Organizational psychology?
Why are you interested in studying industrial-organizational psychology at Sacramento State University?
The GRE requirement for the Industrial-Organizational Psychology program has been waived for the 2024/2025 admissions cycle.
All applicants are required to submit 3 letters of recommendation (preferably Psychology faculty members) with their application.

We suggest that applicants waive their rights to see the letters of recommendation from their letter writers. In this way, letter writers are likely to speak freely, and faculty reviewers are likely to trust the contents of the letters. Waivers must be completed before invitations are sent to letter writers.

The Sacramento State Psychology Graduate Program is not intended for those seeking to perform clinical counseling or mental health therapy. Those seeking such programs should visit the Sacramento State Counselor Education Program or the Masters of Social Work .
Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) program: No.
Industrial-Organizational (I-O) program: The I-O program has waived the GRE requirement for the upcoming admissions cycle (Fall 2025).

You must submit a course description and the syllabi for the courses that you are using to satisfy the Psychology Stats/Methods requirement. You will be asked to upload these materials to the CalState Apply application in Quadrant IV.

No - The Department of Psychology will only consider applications from students who have submitted their completed applications by the stated deadline. Letters of Recommendation must be received by the Department no later than January 15th.

Because of the high volume of applications received by the Department, we are not able to provide status updates to applicants. However, applicants may check the status of their applications on My Sac State in the Student Center. You may contact the Psychology Department to ensure your materials have been received.

International applicants should apply through the International Programs and Global Education department. This will ensure that the Department can review your supplemental documents while IPGE processes your University application. International applicants should submit their applications to IPGE at least 30 days prior to our application deadline. Also, be sure to send transcripts that have been evaluated by World Education Services (WES). Visit International Programs and Global Education for additional information.

pages.title: MS in Applied Behavior Analysis | Sacramento State
pages.description: The Behavior Analysis course sequence at Sacramento State has been approved by the Behavior Analysis Certification Board (BACB) as well as the Association for Behavior Analysis International.
headings: Psychology College of Social Sciences & Interdisciplinary Studies
MS in Applied Behavior Analysis Coursework
Student Association for Applied Behavior Analysis (SAABA)
contentBlocks: The MS in Applied Behavior Analysis Program at Sacramento State is accredited by the Association for Behavior Analysis International Accreditation Board.

Applied behavior analysis (ABA) is the field of Psychology concerned with understanding the environmental variables that reliably influence human behavior, as well as developing procedures to modify socially significant behavior.

The mission of the Master of Science in Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) program is to prepare students to (a) think critically about behavioral processes across various socially meaningful issues, populations, and practice areas, (b) comprehend and contribute to behavior-analytic scholarship, (c) enter the ABA workforce by passing the necessary certification and/or licensure examinations to practice as a behavior analyst, (d) serve diverse communities as Master’s level scientist-practitioners, and (e) enter doctoral training programs in behavior analysis.

The ABA program prepares students to work as behavior analysts in a variety of settings including schools and agencies providing services to individuals with disabilities. The ABA program coursework fulfills the requirements to sit for the national certification exam in Behavior Analysis (BCBA). Students should note that this degree option is also appropriate for those planning to enter doctoral programs in Applied Behavior Analysis, Experimental Analysis of Behavior, or Special Education.
The Behavior Analysis Program at Sacramento State has 3 goals:
To teach students to think critically and scientifically about behavioral processes;
To enable students to enter doctoral programs in behavior analysis;
To prepare students for careers in applied behavior analysis by passing the national certification examination (BCABA and BCBA).
Applied Behavior Analysis Overview Video
Dr. Caio Miguel's Interview with Applied Behavior Analysis EDU
Admission Requirements and Application Instructions
Information on Licensure for Behavior Analysts
Denys Brand, Ph.D. (University of Auckland, New Zealand)
Lab Website: Performance Improvement Lab
Research Interests: Performance feedback, performance management, sequential analysis, procedural integrity, three-alternative choice
Megan Heinicke, Ph.D. (Auburn University)
Research Interests: Stimulus Preference Assessments, Acquired Brain Injury Rehabilitation, Developmental Disabilities, Verbal Behavior, Teaching of Behavior Analysis
Ciobha McKeown, Ph.D . (University of Nebraska Medical Center)
Lab Website: Infant, Toddler, and Life Skills Lab
Research Interests: Autism, Early Intensive Behavioral Intervention, Infant/Toddler Development, Prevention, Social Skills, Verbal Behavior
Caio Miguel, Ph.D. (Western Michigan University)
Research Interests: Verbal Behavior, Stimulus Control/Equivalence, Autism Treatment, Applied Animal Behavior
Becky Penrod, Ph.D. (University of Nevada, Reno)
Lab Website: Pediatric Behavior Research Lab
Research Interests: Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA), Pediatric Feeding Disorders, Assessment and Treatment of Problem Behavior, and Behavioral Health

Students in the ABA Program have the opportunity to engage in state of the art research on a variety of applied and basic topics. Our research facilities include human and animal (rat) operant laboratories and research rooms for data collection with children and adults. We have also established research partnerships with different agencies in the region.

Clinical training is an integral part of our program. There are numerous funded internships and job opportunities for behavior analysis students in sacramento to obtain clinical experience working under the direction of Board Certified Behavior Analysts. Examples of internships* include:
ACES, Comprehensive Educational Services, Inc. - Off-campus program providing center-based and in-home behavioral intervention and language therapy services for children diagnosed with autism.
Behavior Analytic Solutions, LLC. - Off-campus program providing behavioral intervention services for individuals diagnosed with autism.
B.E.S.T Consulting, LLC - Off-campus program providing in-home behavioral intervention services for children diagnosed with autism.
H.O.P.E Consulting, LLC - Off-campus program providing in-home and center-based behavioral intervention services for children and adults diagnosed with autism.
Pacific Autism Learning Services - Off-campus program providing in-home and center-based behavioral intervention services for children and adults diagnosed with autism.
Positive Behavior Support Corporation - Off-campus program providing behavioral intervention services for children and adults diagnosed with autism in-home, schools, and community settings.
* Assistantships and stipends are available for these positions.
Transfer Credit Policy (Graduate Courses)

The M.S. in ABA program at Sacramento State has been verified by the Association for Behavior Analysis International as meeting the coursework requirements for taking the Board Certified Behavior Analyst (BCBA) examination.

SAABA’s mission is to promote the study of Applied Behavior Analysis by providing its members with opportunities in the field through social networking, workshops, conferences, and guest lecturer events.

pages.title: Applying to the Psychology Major | Sacramento State
pages.description: This page provides students with information about the Psychology Major's application process.
headings: Psychology College of Social Sciences & Interdisciplinary Studies
Program Learning Outcomes for the Psychology Major
Steps for admission to the Psychology major
Roadmap of When to Complete Coursework
contentBlocks: We are taking steps to come off of impaction and will launch our Pre-Major beginning in the Fall 2026 admissions cycle. Please read the following information carefully to understand how best to prepare yourself and to understand the process of becoming a Psychology major at Sacramento State.

Psychology courses are offered mostly in-person with about 20% of class options online or in hybrid format every semester, and you can also take one online class each term through the CSU Fully Online program if you are enrolled in at least 7 units at Sacramento State. However, the major itself cannot be completed entirely online. If you need a fully online option, the College of Continuing Education (CCE) offers a Psychology Degree Completion Program with the same curriculum and faculty as the traditional program. This program is designed for working professionals who have completed their general education requirements and are returning to finish their bachelor’s degree.

The CAL State Apply applications for Fall 2026 admissions opened on October 1, 2025. This is before the 2026-2027 CSUS catalog is published, therefore, we have included important information for you here.

California State University - Sacramento has approved the “Pre-major” as a requirement for students for admission in the Psychology major. The Pre-Major will go in effect Fall 2026. All applicants, including transfer students, must be admitted to the University prior to applying to the major.
To become a Psychology major, the student must first have successfully completed the following courses or their equivalent with a grade of "C -" or better:
Submit application to California State University - Sacramento, choosing Psychology and acknowledging Pre-Major.
Upon acceptance to the University, complete all prerequisite coursework completed with a grade of "C -" or better.

Students must have the courses completed (grades must be posted to their transcript) before acceptance to the Psychology major will be processed. In-progress courses will only be accepted for transfer students completing their ADT (Associate Degree for Transfer). This exception is only for ADTs in Psychology, not other subjects.

Once all courses have been completed and grades have been posted to student's transcript, declare the Psychology major by completing the eligibility form linked here and in the button below. Once Pre-Major coursework is verified to be completed, a Change of Major form will be submitted on the student's behalf by the SSIS Student Success Center. Processing will take 2-3 weeks from time of form submission.

Please note that during Spring 2026 Expressed Interest Psychology (EI PSYC) is the designation for those interested in the Psychology major but not declared Psychology majors yet. In Summer 2026 EI PSYC students will be switched to the Pre-Major PSYC declaration. This change will occur once the 2026-2027 catalog is active. Eligibility Form
Visit the SSIS Student Success Center for support and help navigating the process to becoming a PSYC major. SSIS Student Success Center

Students transferring from a California community college should consult ASSIST.org to determine course equivalencies that satisfy Sacramento State Psychology prerequisite requirements. To see a list of approved and no longer approved courses equivalent to PSYC 9 from other institutions, view the linked PSYC 9 Course Substitution List .
Note, most upper division Psychology courses are restricted to Psychology majors or minors.
Program Learning Outcomes for the Psychology Major
Applying Information for Fall 2026 & Beyond

links.text: Program Learning Outcomes for the Psychology Major

pages.title: MS in Psychological Research | Sacramento State
headings: Psychology College of Social Sciences & Interdisciplinary Studies
This program was updated from an MA in General Psychology to an MS in Psychological Research in Fall 2024.
contentBlocks: Students in the Psychological Research program can create individual programs in conjunction with the faculty to prepare for doctoral programs and careers in a variety of specialized fields such as:
Clinical ( Clinically-oriented students in this program will train in clinical research, but will not receive formal clinical skills training or earn licensure ).
Students also take graduate courses in research methods, analysis of variance, multiple regression analysis, multivariate analysis, and psychological measurement.
Psychological Research Program Catalog Information

Psychological Research M.S. students regularly participate in supervisory and experiential coursework beginning in their first semester and continue each semester after that. These students involve themselves in research projects, either as members of research teams or individually under the supervision of a faculty member.

Upon completion, they present their work at a variety of research conferences including the Sacramento Valley Psychological Association's Student Research Conference, Western Psychological Association Research Conference, and other local, regional, and national meetings.

Students may also have the opportunity to acquire experience as Teaching Associates (paid instructors of record) or as Instructional Student Assistants (paid) in our undergraduate lower division and lab courses.
Admission Requirements & Application Instructions

Applicants will be asked to select and rank three faculty from the list below in their application. Please review our application materials to determine which faculty will be taking applicants for Fall 2025.

pages.title: Master’s Degree Program in Industrial-Organizational Psychology | Sacramento State
pages.description: Industrial-Organizational Psychology involves the application of psychology to the workplace. I-O psychologists aim to improve the quality of working life for employees, as well as to help workplaces operate more effectively.
headings: Psychology College of Social Sciences & Interdisciplinary Studies
Master’s Degree Program in Industrial-Organizational Psychology

Where Our Students and Alumni Work
contentBlocks: Welcome – In our program you will learn to apply your psychological knowledge to improve the workplace by earning your I-O Psychology M.A. degree! Applications will be accepted in October 2025 (deadline of January 15, 2026).
Follows the "scientist/practitioner" model, with a combined emphasis on research and practical applications
Designed to complete the I-O Psychology M.A. in 2.5 years
Accommodates working adults; most classes are in the evenings
Alumni work in government, business, and consulting positions
GRE not required for this application cycle
Admission Requirements & Application Instructions

The I-O program at Sacramento State was designed with heavy influence from the 2016 Guidelines for Education and Training in Industrial-Organizational Psychology, published by the Society for Industrial and Organizational Psychology. While we cover all 24 major competency areas in the SIOP Guidelines to some degree, we give more emphasis to some than we do others—a strategy that is common and fully expected in master’s programs according to the narrative of the SIOP Guidelines. Our current curriculum includes the following courses:
Research Methods Classes: PSYC 200: Methods in Empirical Psychology PSYC 202: Survey of Contemporary Statistical Methods in Psychological Research
PSYC 200: Methods in Empirical Psychology
PSYC 202: Survey of Contemporary Statistical Methods in Psychological Research
Measurement Classes: PSYC 206: Tests and Measurement PSYC 205: Measurement Methods for Psychological Research and Practice
PSYC 205: Measurement Methods for Psychological Research and Practice

Core I-O Classes: PSYC 269: Analysis of Work and Employee Selection PSYC 267: Training & Employee Development PSYC 216A: Current Literature and Applications in Industrial-Organizational Psychology PSYC 216B: Current Literature and Applications in Industrial-Organizational Psychology
PSYC 269: Analysis of Work and Employee Selection
PSYC 267: Training & Employee Development
PSYC 216A: Current Literature and Applications in Industrial-Organizational Psychology
PSYC 216B: Current Literature and Applications in Industrial-Organizational Psychology
Elective: PSYC 294: Cooperative Research (or any other 200-level course)
PSYC 294: Cooperative Research (or any other 200-level course)
Culminating Experience: Master’s Project

An undergraduate degree in psychology is strongly preferred. Some general preparatory coursework, especially relevant to prospective applicants who were not psychology majors, is listed in the following document: Relevant Undergraduate Coursework at CSU-Sacramento . In addition to what is listed there, undergraduate coursework in industrial and/or organizational psychology is preferred and coursework in psychological testing is very helpful. While not strictly required for admission, these preferences are in place to help students get the most out of their graduate training. Prior work experience is also helpful, but again not required. We appreciate different types and degrees of preparation for graduate study, whether it be primarily academic or experiential.
Admission Requirements & Application Instructions | Sacramento State

Our curriculum is designed for students to take two graduate-level classes per semester. All of the I-O core classes are held in the late afternoons/early evenings, so that should allow for flexibility with your work hours. However, in the first year there will be one method course in the fall and one in the spring that are typically held earlier in the day (one day per week).
We will not be requiring the GRE for the 2025/2026 application cycle.
We accept students into the program each academic year. The next application deadline will be January 15, 2026, to begin classes in Fall 2026.
We look to accept 7-10 students for each cohort.
No, the CalStateApply system will only allow students to apply to one graduate program per CSU campus.

In addition to our Financial Aid resources, all of the students in our previous cohorts have held relevant jobs while completing their degree requirements. Occasionally the faculty will also have limited access to instructional student assistant funds they can use to hire students for activities like grading student work and development of course materials.
In recent cohorts, 3 out of 4 students have finished all degree requirements on schedule, while overall about 9 out of 10 have completed their degrees.

Our program allows students to opt into one of two tracks: the research (thesis) track and the applied (portfolio) track, depending on their desired focus. Students are required to determine their chosen track within their first year of the program.
No, the MA I-O program is not designated a STEM program.
International students are welcome to apply. Please see the International Graduate Applicants webpage for more information.
An information session is scheduled for November 10th from 5:30 - 6:30pm on Zoom. Please RSVP by emailing Dr. Catherine Warren at catherine.warren@csus.edu . Please see the flyer for more details.
We have compiled a list of employers where current students and alumni work or have worked. If you are an alumnus viewing this page and want to add to the list, please let us know!
California Department of Consumer Affairs
California Contractors State Licensing Board
California Board of State and Community Corrections

pages.title: Participating in Research for Course Credit | Sacramento State
pages.description: The Research Requirement Information Page for the Psychology Department provides information to study participants and researchers.
headings: Psychology College of Social Sciences & Interdisciplinary Studies
Participating in Research for Course Credit
contentBlocks: Each of these courses include a research requirement designed to provide students with some understanding of the methods and procedures used to conduct psychological research. To satisfy this requirement, students must complete research work by either participating in actual psychology research studies conducted by Psychology faculty and students or completing an alternative writing assignment.

Students will create their own account using the research website, SONA, and navigate various studies to participate in for credit towards their course grade. Students will select the appropriate course name to allocate credit towards that course. Studies are being created at the beginning of each semester, so as the semester progresses more studies will become available and will conclude before Finals Week. Studies are typically made available for participant sign-up around the 7th or 8th week of the semester. If you do not meet the eligibility requirements of a study, it may not be viewable for you to sign-up for.

Failure to complete the research requirements before the end of the semester will result in a final course grade of "I" or Incomplete. Students will need to complete their remaining credits within one year and assign these credits to the course name "Students Making Up Incompletes". Upon completion of these remaining credits, students need to email psychology@csus.edu to confirm they have completed their credits. After 1 year of not completing the research requirements, the course grade will change from an "I" to an "F".
The last day to participate in research for the Spring 2026 is Friday, May 15th
PSYC 2 and 8 students are required to complete 3 hours of research participation for each class.
PSYC 4 students are required to complete 2 hours of research participation.
To complete the required hours, participants must:
Register for an account or login to an existing account on the SONA - Psychology Research website
Complete the Prescreen after logging in to Sona to help determine your eligibility for research studies.

Choose the correct Course Title that earned credits will be assigned to. If you are not enrolled in a course but are making up credits from a previous semester, choose "1. Students Making Up Incompletes in SP26." To change the course(s) navigate to the "My Profile" tab.
Sign up for study participation. Cancel your sign-up if you cannot complete it, including online studies. Failure to appear to or complete 2 study sign-ups will result in the loss of future sign-ups.

In-Person Studies: arrive with your 5-digit Identity Code handy (for online studies fill it out in the survey when/if prompted). Credits are assigned with the use of Sona Identity Codes and not participant names. This Identity Code is listed under the "My Profile" tab.
Complete required number of research hours before end of semester. (PSYC 2 & 8 = 3 hours each . PSYC 4 = 2 hours)

Monitor credit earnings online and make sure requirements are met by finals week. Study availability fluctuates, so it's best to check weekly for available timeslots as options become very limited at the end of the semester.
Ensure credit is granted and assigned to the correct course, if enrolled in more than one course. Reassign credits to preferred course before end of semester.

Students Making Up Incompletes: select course name "1. Students Making Up Incompletes in SP26" to assign credit for a previous semester. Select "My Profile" to change the course title, if needed. When credits are made up: send an email with your name, student ID number, course name and semester that course was taken to the Psychology Department at psychology@csus.edu asking for assistance with changing your grade. This process is not automatic and will take time for processing.
For study questions/issues: contact the Researcher by email through Sona. *Only the Researcher can grant credit.

Students who have unexcused no-shows for study sign-ups and/or students not wishing to participate in research have the option of completing an alternative writing assignment. Students who complete this assignment will demonstrate their understanding of research methods by reviewing reports of research studies that have recently been published in Psychology research journals.
This alternative writing assignment contains three parts. All parts must be completed satisfactorily in order to receive credit. Carefully follow the detailed instructions below for the assignment.
Make sure you are signed into your CSUS Office365 account when submitting your Alternative Writing Assignment.
The Human Subjects Committee reviews alternative assignments once at the end of each semester and inform your instructor the results of our evaluation at the end of the finals week.

If the committee determines that revisions are required, instructions and a deadline will be sent to your Sacramento State email account. It is your responsibility to monitor your official Sac State email for any follow-up .
Contact Dr. Qin if you have any questions.
Alternative Writing Assignment Instructions
Contact, Dr. J.J. Qin , the current Chair of the Human Subjects Committee for Alternative Writing Assignment inquiries.
Contact psychology@csus.edu to resolve "Incomplete" grades after making up previous semester course requirements.

links.text: Participating in Research for Course Credit

pages.title: Student Researcher Information | Sacramento State
pages.description: Instructions for students conducting human subjects research in the Psychology Department.
headings: Psychology College of Social Sciences & Interdisciplinary Studies
contentBlocks: Students in the Sacramento State Psychology Department who conduct class projects for PSYC 102 or human subjects research must have their proposed methods reviewed and approved prior to recruiting participants and collecting data. PSYC 102 class projects are reviewed and approved by Psychology Department Research Review Committee. Student research conducted by graduate students (e.g., for theses, PSYC 294, 297, etc.) or undergraduate students (e.g., for PSYC 194, 197, etc.) must be approved by University IRB. Follow the instructions below for PSYC 102 class projects and student research.
Review the Researcher Conduct Guidelines .
Use these templates to create a Consent Form and Debriefing Form .

Have your instructor review and approve your project. *If using Qualtrics, configure your settings to ensure that participants successfully complete the survey by requiring a response to each question. This will prevent a participant from clicking through the survey without answering each question and not providing useful data.

Assemble all your research materials. Including all tests, surveys, vignettes, images, etc. that will be used in your study For photos provide the source of the photo if in the public domain; if not in the public domain have individuals in the photos sign a waiver to have their images used for your research. For videos, provide a URL where we can view them; if this is not possible then arrange to have a CD or DVD delivered to Dr. J.J. Qin , the Psychology Department Human Subjects Chair.
Including all tests, surveys, vignettes, images, etc. that will be used in your study
For photos provide the source of the photo if in the public domain; if not in the public domain have individuals in the photos sign a waiver to have their images used for your research.
For videos, provide a URL where we can view them; if this is not possible then arrange to have a CD or DVD delivered to Dr. J.J. Qin , the Psychology Department Human Subjects Chair.
Submit your materials to the Submission Portal by Friday April 4, 2026 .
Wait . The Human Subjects Chair will email you to let you know whether your project is approved or needs to be revised. The review process may take up to 10 days.

The Sona Administrator will assign you the role of "Researcher" after the Census Date. Log in to an existing Sona account using this weblink Sona - Psychology Research. If you are using the subject pool, set up your study in Sona. Use the title, description, and other project information EXACTLY as submitted in your approved application. Any changes will need to be approved first!
In Sona, send a message to the Sona administrator to obtain approval and include your approved number of hours in your message. After your request has been processed, add timeslots and run sessions.

Assign credits to participants promptly after each session that completed the study to your standards. If using a Qualtrics survey, ensure that your survey is set-up to automatically grant credits when survey is completed and consider making some or all responses a requirement within the survey settings.
Deactivate the project in Sona - Psychology Research when no more sessions will be run.
If you are not using the subject pool, you can begin recruiting participants once your proposal has been approved. Follow the procedures approved in your application.

Student researchers who conduct research projects that collect data from human participants must have their proposed methods reviewed and approved by Univeristy IRB before recruiting participants and beginning their projects.
Students planning on recruiting human participants from the Psychology Department's human participant pool should follow the steps below.
Complete the CITI training module; Human Subjects Research: Undergraduate and Masters Students

Submit your research proposal for review to the University's IRB . *If using Qualtrics, configure your settings to ensure that participants successfully complete the survey by requiring a response to each question. This will avoid a participant from clicking through the survey without answering each question and not providing useful data.
After your research is approved by University IRB, complete this brief form to request participants from the subject pool.

Log in to an existing Sona account or create a new Sona account using this weblink Sona - Psychology Research if you plan on using the Psychology Department's human participant pool. Once you are logged in, send a message to the listed Sona Administrator at the bottom of the research webpage with your request of being assigned the "Researcher" role.

After obtaining the researcher role, use the title, description, approval code and other project information EXACTLY as shown in your approved application to set up your study. Any changes will need to be approved first! When you're study is ready in Sona and you have been allocated the approved number of hours, add timeslots and run sessions. The final day for participants to complete your study is the Friday before Finals Week.
Assign credit to participants that completed your study appropriately promptly after each session.
Deactivate your project in Sona - Psychology Research when no more sessions will be run.

Please note that researchers may not be allocated the total number of requested participants in a single semester. Researchers requesting large samples may need to collect data over several semesters.
Detailed instructions for submitting class projects for review.
How to use the Psychology Research website
Psychology Department Human Subjects Committee Chair: Dr. J.J. Qin .
